@@ -0,0 +1,33 @@
+---
+assignees:
+- wballard
+position_column: todo
+position_ordinal: f780
+title: Wire validator-side tools into llama-agent (file/code_context schemas in chat template)
+---
+**Why this is the upstream cause of qwen's failed tool calls.** Qwen3.6's chat template (`tokenizer_config.json`) only injects `<tool_call>...</tool_call>` Hermes tool schemas when tools are passed in to the inference call. With no tools registered, the model has nothing to call against — and improvises a tool-call shape (we saw `{"call_tool": {"name": "...", "arguments": "<stringified>"}}` wrapped in markdown fences). No tool parser, however lenient, can rescue a model that's hallucinating a schema. The fix is to register tools, and the chat template will then put the model on rails.
+
+**Where it lives:**
+
+- `avp-common/src/context.rs::resolve_validator_tools` (around line 316) is the validator-side gate. Today it returns `(None, None)` unless `SAH_HTTP_PORT` / `SWISSARMYHAMMER_HTTP_PORT` is set, on the comment grounds of "the validator runs without tools (prompt-only, backward compatible)." That path is the wrong default. When the executor agent is claude-code, claude has its own built-in Read/Grep/etc., so the empty tool registry on our side is invisible — claude works fine. When the executor is llama-agent (qwen3.6), there are no built-in tools, so an empty registry leaves the model with literally nothing to call. The validator prompts advertise `files` and `code_context`; we're lying to the model about what's available.
+- llama-agent receives tools through whatever path `resolve_validator_tools` routes them. Track the type signature on the agent side (`llama-agent/src/acp/server.rs` and `mcp.rs` look like the relevant call sites) and confirm tools registered there land in the chat template's `tools=[...]` rendering.
+
+**What needs to happen:**
+
+1. **Provide a default validator tool set without requiring `SAH_HTTP_PORT`.** Read-only files plus code_context (sah's existing tools): `Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`, `code_context::get_symbol`, `code_context::search_symbol`, `code_context::list_symbols`, `code_context::grep_code`, `code_context::get_callgraph`, `code_context::get_blastradius`. Match what the rule prompts already advertise in their "Available Tools" section, so the prompt and the registry agree.
+2. **Pass these tools into llama-agent at validator session creation.** When `runner.rs` calls `agent.new_session(...)` for a rule, the resulting session must have the tool list registered such that the next `prompt()` call's chat template injects them. If llama-agent currently expects MCP-server tools (HTTP), we either (a) wire an in-process MCP server for the validator subprocess, or (b) extend llama-agent to accept a direct `Vec<ToolDef>` at session start. (b) is cleaner; (a) reuses the existing path.
+3. **Keep claude-code working unchanged.** Don't push our own tools at claude — it has its own. The default-tools wiring only kicks in for the llama-agent executor.
+4. **Per-RuleSet override is fine but not required.** A `VALIDATOR.md` field like `tools: [files, code_context]` (or `tools: []` to opt out) is a reasonable affordance later. For this task, ship the default set; defer configurability until someone needs it.
+
+**Acceptance:**
+
+- With qwen3.6 as the validator agent and no `SAH_HTTP_PORT` set, run a Stop-hook validation against a deliberately bad Rust file (magic numbers, hardcoded IPs, hardcoded `return 42`). Confirm in `.avp/log` that at least one rule actually invokes `read_file` (or equivalent) on the target file, the tool result is fed back, and the rule returns a verdict that references actual file content. No more `Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning`.
+- claude-code-as-validator continues to work end-to-end, unchanged.
+- A regression test asserts that the validator session creation passes a non-empty tool list to llama-agent under default config.
+
+**Pairs with:** `01KQ35KFJXJ70GNB4ZPRJD6R43` (lenient parser). Wire tools first, then a parser that handles the canonical Hermes `<tool_call>...</tool_call>` output the model will then actually emit. Either order works for testing the other in isolation, but the end-to-end qwen-as-validator path needs both.
+
+**Sources:**
+- [Qwen Function Calling docs — chat template tool injection](https://qwen.readthedocs.io/en/latest/framework/function_call.html)
+- [Qwen3.6 official blog](https://qwen.ai/blog?id=qwen3.6-27b)
+- [vLLM Tool Calling — `--enable-auto-tool-choice` mechanics](https://docs.vllm.ai/en/latest/features/tool_calling/)
\ No newline at end of file

-**Why this is the upstream cause of qwen's failed tool calls.** Qwen3.6's chat template (`tokenizer_config.json`) only injects `<tool_call>...</tool_call>` Hermes tool schemas when tools are passed in to the inference call. With no tools registered, the model has nothing to call against — and improvises a tool-call shape (we saw `{"call_tool": {"name": "...", "arguments": "<stringified>"}}` wrapped in markdown fences). No tool parser, however lenient, can rescue a model that's hallucinating a schema. The fix is to register tools, and the chat template will then put the model on rails.
+**Design recap (correcting an earlier mis-statement of mine):** The validator tool path is:

-**Where it lives:**
+1. Parent claude starts sah as an MCP server with HTTP transport. sah binds a port and exports `SAH_HTTP_PORT` (or `SWISSARMYHAMMER_HTTP_PORT`).
+2. Claude's hooks fire avp as a subprocess. avp inherits the env, including `SAH_HTTP_PORT`.
+3. `avp-common/src/context.rs::resolve_validator_mcp_config` reads the port and returns `Some(McpServerConfig { url: "http://localhost:{port}/mcp/validator" })` plus `tools_override = Some("")` (the override disables agent-builtin tools so the validator uses *only* sah's MCP-served ones).
+4. The validator agent (claude-code or llama-agent) is constructed with that MCP config. For claude-code, this swaps claude's built-in tools for sah's ones. For llama-agent, this is the only path tools can arrive on.
+5. When llama-agent calls the model, the chat template (`tokenizer_config.json` for the chosen model) renders the tools list as Hermes-style tool schemas in the system message, and the model emits `<tool_call>...</tool_call>` JSON when it wants to call one.

-- `avp-common/src/context.rs::resolve_validator_tools` (around line 316) is the validator-side gate. Today it returns `(None, None)` unless `SAH_HTTP_PORT` / `SWISSARMYHAMMER_HTTP_PORT` is set, on the comment grounds of "the validator runs without tools (prompt-only, backward compatible)." That path is the wrong default. When the executor agent is claude-code, claude has its own built-in Read/Grep/etc., so the empty tool registry on our side is invisible — claude works fine. When the executor is llama-agent (qwen3.6), there are no built-in tools, so an empty registry leaves the model with literally nothing to call. The validator prompts advertise `files` and `code_context`; we're lying to the model about what's available.
-- llama-agent receives tools through whatever path `resolve_validator_tools` routes them. Track the type signature on the agent side (`llama-agent/src/acp/server.rs` and `mcp.rs` look like the relevant call sites) and confirm tools registered there land in the chat template's `tools=[...]` rendering.
+The `(None, None)` branch — env var unset — is a deliberate backward-compat path. Claude falls back to its own built-in Read/Grep, so claude-as-validator just works. llama-agent has no fallback, so it runs prompt-only when the var is missing. **That branch is not a bug; it is the design.** The earlier framing of this task as "wrong default" was wrong.

-**What needs to happen:**
+**The real questions, and what this task is to figure out and fix:**

-1. **Provide a default validator tool set without requiring `SAH_HTTP_PORT`.** Read-only files plus code_context (sah's existing tools): `Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`, `code_context::get_symbol`, `code_context::search_symbol`, `code_context::list_symbols`, `code_context::grep_code`, `code_context::get_callgraph`, `code_context::get_blastradius`. Match what the rule prompts already advertise in their "Available Tools" section, so the prompt and the registry agree.
-2. **Pass these tools into llama-agent at validator session creation.** When `runner.rs` calls `agent.new_session(...)` for a rule, the resulting session must have the tool list registered such that the next `prompt()` call's chat template injects them. If llama-agent currently expects MCP-server tools (HTTP), we either (a) wire an in-process MCP server for the validator subprocess, or (b) extend llama-agent to accept a direct `Vec<ToolDef>` at session start. (b) is cleaner; (a) reuses the existing path.
-3. **Keep claude-code working unchanged.** Don't push our own tools at claude — it has its own. The default-tools wiring only kicks in for the llama-agent executor.
-4. **Per-RuleSet override is fine but not required.** A `VALIDATOR.md` field like `tools: [files, code_context]` (or `tools: []` to opt out) is a reasonable affordance later. For this task, ship the default set; defer configurability until someone needs it.
+1. **Is `SAH_HTTP_PORT` actually set when avp runs from the Stop hook in a normal claude session?** Confirm by adding temporary `tracing::info!` to `resolve_validator_mcp_config` that logs `port: {value-or-none}` and `mcp_url: {value-or-none}` at validator-agent construction time, then re-run the qwen Stop-hook test and read `.avp/log`. If the var is missing, find out where the propagation breaks: is sah running stdio MCP only (the .mcp.json in this repo says `command: sah, args: [serve]`, which is typically stdio)? Does sah need an HTTP transport mode to export the port? Is there a different launch path that sets it? Document the answer.

+2. **If the port is set but qwen still doesn't get usable tool schemas:** the bug is downstream of `resolve_validator_mcp_config`. Trace: `McpServerConfig` → llama-agent session construction → does llama-agent fetch the tool list from `/mcp/validator` over HTTP? → does it render those tools through the Qwen3 chat template's `tools=[...]` section? → does the chat template wrap them in Hermes `<tool_call>` schema? Each step is a possible failure point. Likely culprits to inspect first: `llama-agent/src/acp/server.rs`, `llama-agent/src/mcp.rs`, and how the chat template's `tools` arg is populated for non-OpenAI strategies.
+
+3. **If the port is NOT set and we want llama-agent-as-validator to work in default setups:** decide between (a) make sah always export `SAH_HTTP_PORT` when it's running (start an HTTP transport alongside stdio if needed), or (b) wire an in-process MCP path so llama-agent can get tools without an HTTP hop. (a) is more consistent with the existing design and reuses the validator endpoint that already exists. (b) is more efficient and doesn't require a port. User preference here matters.
+
 **Acceptance:**

-- With qwen3.6 as the validator agent and no `SAH_HTTP_PORT` set, run a Stop-hook validation against a deliberately bad Rust file (magic numbers, hardcoded IPs, hardcoded `return 42`). Confirm in `.avp/log` that at least one rule actually invokes `read_file` (or equivalent) on the target file, the tool result is fed back, and the rule returns a verdict that references actual file content. No more `Validator returned unparseable response, passing with warning`.
-- claude-code-as-validator continues to work end-to-end, unchanged.
-- A regression test asserts that the validator session creation passes a non-empty tool list to llama-agent under default config.
+- The actual cause of qwen's tool failure on 2026-04-25 is identified and documented in this task's notes (env var not set, MCP path broken, chat template not Hermes-ifying tools, or some combination).
+- With qwen3.6 as the validator agent in the user's normal setup (claude → sah-as-MCP-server → avp-as-hook → llama-agent), at least one rule actually invokes a tool and the result is fed back. Confirm in `.avp/log`.
+- claude-code-as-validator continues to work end-to-end, unchanged. No regressions in the `(None, None)` fallback path.

-**Pairs with:** `01KQ35KFJXJ70GNB4ZPRJD6R43` (lenient parser). Wire tools first, then a parser that handles the canonical Hermes `<tool_call>...</tool_call>` output the model will then actually emit. Either order works for testing the other in isolation, but the end-to-end qwen-as-validator path needs both.
+**Pairs with:** `01KQ35KFJXJ70GNB4ZPRJD6R43` (lenient parser). Once tools are flowing, qwen should emit canonical Hermes `<tool_call>{...}</tool_call>` and the parser must handle it.

 **Sources:**
 - [Qwen Function Calling docs — chat template tool injection](https://qwen.readthedocs.io/en/latest/framework/function_call.html)

+## What avp does on every hook invocation

-1. Parent claude (or any host) starts sah as an MCP server with HTTP transport. sah binds a port and exports `SAH_HTTP_PORT` (or `SWISSARMYHAMMER_HTTP_PORT`).
-2. Claude's hooks fire avp as a subprocess. avp inherits the env, including `SAH_HTTP_PORT`.
-3. `avp-common/src/context.rs::resolve_validator_mcp_config` reads the port and returns `Some(McpServerConfig { url: "http://localhost:{port}/mcp/validator" })` plus `tools_override = Some("")`.
-4. The validator agent (claude-code or llama-agent) is constructed with that MCP config.
-5. For llama-agent, the chat template reads tools via the MCP client and renders them into the system message — handled in llama-agent (see `01KQ35KFJXJ70GNB4ZPRJD6R43`).
+1. `AvpContext::init` calls `swissarmyhammer_tools::mcp::start_validator_mcp_server(...)` (new function — see below). Returns an `McpServerHandle` bound on `127.0.0.1:0`.
+2. The handle's `.url()` is plugged into `McpServerConfig` for the validator agent. `tools_override = Some("")` so claude-as-validator drops its built-in tools and uses ours.
+3. The handle is stored on `AvpContext`. Drop runs `shutdown()`.

-The current `(None, None)` branch (env var unset) leaves llama-agent with no tools at all. Claude-as-validator falls back to its own built-in Read/Grep so it doesn't notice; qwen has no such fallback.
+That's it. No env-var checks, no branches, no warn logs about fallback firing. The validator MCP server is part of avp's process, started fresh each time, registered with the narrow tool set the validators are allowed to see.

-## What to change
+## Tool set: read-only files + code_context only

-**If `SAH_HTTP_PORT` is set:** keep the existing path. No change.
+Validators are pure judgment processes — they don't write, they don't shell out, they don't manage kanban or git. They read code and decide. The exposed tool set is:

-**If `SAH_HTTP_PORT` is unset:** start an in-process sah MCP server. **Do not shell out a subprocess.** The required entry point already exists in this workspace:
-
-```rust

-pub async fn start_mcp_server_with_options(
-    mode: McpServerMode,                     // McpServerMode::Http { port: None } → bind 127.0.0.1:0
-    library: Option<PromptLibrary>,          // None — defaults are fine
-    model_override: Option<String>,          // None
-    working_dir: Option<std::path::PathBuf>, // Some(project_root)
